title: Connecting Kafka to ClickHouse with SSL: A Complete Integration Guide
subtitle: Setting up a production-ready data pipeline requires balancing high performance with rigorous security. When integrating ClickHouse with a…
excerpt: Setting up a production-ready data pipeline requires balancing high performance with rigorous security. When integrating ClickHouse with a…

content.html: Setting up a production-ready data pipeline requires balancing high performance with rigorous security. When integrating ClickHouse with a Kafka cluster secured by SASL_SSL, engineers often hit a “wall” trying to pass SSL certificates directly through SQL commands.
This guide provides a definitive walkthrough for configuring server-side security, building the ingestion pipeline, and tuning the engine for performance.

content.alt: Connecting Kafka to ClickHouse with SSL: A Complete Integration Guide

content.html: The ClickHouse Kafka engine acts as a “buffer.” To move data into permanent storage, you need three distinct objects working in tandem:

items: Kafka Engine Table: A virtual table that acts as a live consumer/pointer to the Kafka stream.
Materialized View: The “glue” that triggers consumption and handles data transformation.
MergeTree Table: The final destination where data is physically stored and indexed for analytics.

content.html: The most common error when setting up this integration is Code: 115 (UNKNOWN_SETTING). This happens because ClickHouse (as of v25.8) does not allow library-level SSL configurations — like kafka_ssl_ca_location—to be "inlined" in a CREATE TABLE statement.
Place your Kafka CA certificate on the ClickHouse server. The clickhouse user must have explicit read access.

content.code: sudo mkdir -p /etc/clickhouse-server/certssudo cp your-ca.pem /etc/clickhouse-server/certs/kafka-ca.pemsudo chown clickhouse:clickhouse /etc/clickhouse-server/certs/kafka-ca.pemsudo chmod 440 /etc/clickhouse-server/certs/kafka-ca.pem

content.html: By placing credentials in a configuration file rather than SQL, you keep sensitive data out of query logs and system metadata. Create a new file in the config.d directory:
Note: Ensure the XML tags match your broker’s required mechanism (e.g., PLAIN, SCRAM-SHA-256).
Restart the server to apply changes and use openssl to ensure the ClickHouse host can reach the broker over port 9093:
With security handled at the server level, your SQL code remains clean and focused on the data schema.
This is your “Source of Truth,” optimized for analytical queries.

content.code: CREATE TABLE events_destination (    event_id    UInt64,    event_name  String,    event_time  DateTime,    payload     String,    ingested_at DateTime DEFAULT now()) ENGINE = MergeTree()ORDER BY (event_time, event_id);

content.html: This table defines the connection to the topic. Because of our XML config, we don’t need to specify SSL details here.

content.code: CREATE TABLE kafka_queue_stream (    event_id    UInt64,    event_name  String,    event_time  DateTime,    payload     String) ENGINE = KafkaSETTINGS    kafka_broker_list = 'ip:9093,ip:9093',    kafka_topic_list = 'your_topic_name',    kafka_group_name = 'clickhouse_consumer_v1',    kafka_format = 'JSONEachRow',    kafka_num_consumers = 4, -- Matches Kafka partitions for parallel reading    kafka_skip_broken_messages = 1;

content.html: Once created, this object starts the background consumption process.

content.code: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW kafka_ingest_mv TO events_destination ASSELECT event_id, event_name, event_time, payloadFROM kafka_queue_stream;

content.alt: Connecting Kafka to ClickHouse with SSL: A Complete Integration Guide
Connecting Kafka to ClickHouse with SSL: A Complete Integration Guide

content.html: By separating infrastructure concerns (SSL/SASL in XML) from application concerns (schemas and topics in SQL), you create a robust, secure, and maintainable pipeline. This architecture ensures that sensitive credentials stay out of logs while maintaining the high-performance “at-least-once” delivery guarantees ClickHouse is known for.
